informal_statement.text: Given a slice of closed-open intervals [start, end), return the maximum number of mutually non-overlapping intervals selectable from the input.
preconditions: for every input interval, start < end
required_properties: the returned count equals the size of some subset of pairwise non-overlapping intervals from the input
no subset of pairwise non-overlapping intervals from the input is strictly larger than the returned count
intervals sharing only an endpoint (interval i ends where interval j starts) are considered non-overlapping
edge_cases: all intervals mutually disjoint (result equals len)
all intervals overlap a common point (result is 1)
intervals touching at a single endpoint

semantic_units.description: Input precondition: every interval satisfies start < stop.
Feasibility: the returned count is witnessed by some subset of the input that is pairwise non-overlapping.
common_failure_modes: confusing index selections with value selections

semantic_units.description: Optimality: no pairwise non-overlapping subset of the input is strictly larger than the returned count.
common_failure_modes: stating only feasibility (admits the trivial return 0)

semantic_units.description: Endpoint convention: two intervals that share only an endpoint are non-overlapping.
common_failure_modes: using strict inequality instead of non-strict at the boundary

semantic_units.description: Empty input: the result is 0 when the input is empty.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (ivs : Intervals), (∀ i ∈ ivs, i.start < i.stop) → ∃ (sel : List Nat), Feasible ivs sel ∧ sel.length = intervalScheduling ivs
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When every input interval has strictly positive length, there exists a feasible selection whose size equals the returned count.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (ivs : Intervals) (sel : List Nat), (∀ i ∈ ivs, i.start < i.stop) → Feasible ivs sel → sel.length ≤ intervalScheduling ivs
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When every input interval has strictly positive length, no feasible selection is strictly larger than the returned count.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Interval), a.stop = b.start → NonOverlap a b
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: Intervals touching at a single endpoint are non-overlapping.

generated_obligations.nl_gloss: No explicit preconditions are asserted by the implementation; it accepts any list of (start,end) integer pairs.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def nonoverlap (iv₁ iv₂ : Int × Int) : Prop := iv₁.1 ≥ iv₂.2 ∨ iv₂.1 ≥ iv₁.2
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Intervals are treated as half-open [start,end). Two intervals are non-overlapping iff one starts at or after the other ends.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def feasible (S : List (Int × Int)) : Prop := ∀ ⦃a b⦄, a ∈ S → b ∈ S → a ≠ b → nonoverlap a b
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: A set/list of intervals is feasible if every distinct pair is non-overlapping.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
def greedySelect (sorted : List (Int × Int)) : List (Int × Int) :=
  let rec go (lastEnd : Option Int) (acc : List (Int × Int)) (xs : List (Int × Int)) : List (Int × Int) :=
    match xs with
    | [] => acc
    | (s,e)::xs' =>
      match lastEnd with
      | none => go (some e) ((s,e)::acc) xs'
      | some le => if s ≥ le then go (some e) ((s,e)::acc) xs' else go lastEnd acc xs'
  go none [] sorted
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Model the loop: after sorting by increasing end, greedily take an interval iff its start is ≥ the end of the last chosen interval.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def greedyCount (intervals : List (Int × Int)) : Nat := (greedySelect (intervals.sort (fun a b => a.2 ≤ b.2))).length
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Model the returned count as the length of the greedy-selected intervals after sorting by end time.
The Rust function returns exactly the number of intervals selected by the greedy procedure described above.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (sorted : List (Int × Int)) (acc : List (Int × Int)) (lastEnd : Option Int),
  let chosen := greedySelect sorted in
  True
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Placeholder for the loop invariant structure: the chosen intervals are exactly those accumulated by the greedy rule; the key properties are captured in subsequent obligations.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (sorted : List (Int × Int)),
  let chosen := greedySelect sorted
  in feasible chosen
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: All intervals chosen by the greedy algorithm are mutually non-overlapping (feasibility).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (sorted : List (Int × Int)),
  let chosen := greedySelect sorted
  in ∀ {a b}, a ∈ chosen → b ∈ chosen → a ≠ b →
     (a.2 ≤ b.1 ∨ b.2 ≤ a.1)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Stronger pairwise non-overlap property consistent with half-open intervals: for any two chosen intervals, one ends no later than the other starts.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (sorted : List (Int × Int)),
  let chosen := greedySelect sorted
  in ∀ {a b}, a ∈ chosen → b ∈ chosen → a ≠ b →
     (a.2 ≤ b.2 ∨ b.2 ≤ a.2)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Because the algorithm processes intervals in nondecreasing end-time order, the chosen intervals can be assumed to respect that ordering; this supports exchange arguments.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (intervals : List (Int × Int)),
  let n := interval_scheduling intervals
  in ∃ (S : List (Int × Int)),
     S ⊆ intervals ∧ feasible S ∧ S.length = n
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The algorithm returns the size of some feasible subset of the input intervals (so the count is achievable).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (intervals : List (Int × Int)),
  let n := interval_scheduling intervals
  in ∀ (T : List (Int × Int)),
     T ⊆ intervals → feasible T → T.length ≤ n
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Maximum-cardinality optimality: no feasible subset of the input has more intervals than the greedy algorithm returns.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (sorted : List (Int × Int)),
  let chosen := greedySelect sorted
  in True
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Placeholder obligation to structure the correctness proof via the standard earliest-finish-time exchange argument; the concrete exchange/induction steps would be formalized in additional lemmas in a full development.
